When a craving hits, delaying the response by even 5 to 10 minutes is often enough for it to pass. Tell yourself you will smoke in 10 minutes if you still want to â€” and then do something else for those 10 minutes. Most of the time the urge has weakened significantly by the time the 10 minutes are up.

step2_rag_chunks: There are two kinds of triggers â€” situational and emotional. Situational triggers are external: after meals, with tea, during a work break. Emotional triggers are internal: stress, boredom, loneliness, anxiety. Most people have both. Knowing which type is stronger for you helps us choose the right approach.
Taking a short walk or doing light physical activity can help reduce stress and distract from cravings.
Keeping a diary of triggers and cravings can help identify patterns and develop better coping strategies.

step2_rag_chunks: Cravings for tobacco are temporary and usually last between 5 and 10 minutes. When a craving occurs, delay smoking and focus on another activity until the urge passes.
Reward yourself for milestones such as staying smoke-free for a day, a week, or a month.

Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.

Patient: It feels... good to think about, I guess. I mean, it's nice to imagine being able to say no and feeling proud of myself for quitting. But it's hard to picture actually being in that situation and feeling proud of myself. It feels more like, I don't know... I'd probably just end up smoking again anyway.

Therapist: You're starting to imagine having more control over situations that trigger your desire to smoke, like crowded parties. What if you wrote down specific reasons why quitting is important to you, like the reasons you mentioned earlier, family pressure, weight concerns, and feeling in control, to remind yourself of your motivations when you're in those situations?

Patient: .. I don't know if that would really help. I mean, it's not like I forget why I want to quit. It's just... it feels hard to actually do it, you know? But if it would help, I could try writing them down, I guess.
Current patient message:

.. I don't know if that would really help. I mean, it's not like I forget why I want to quit. It's just... it feels hard to actually do it, you know? But if it would help, I could try writing them down, I guess.

step7_response: It sounds like you're feeling uncertain about whether writing down your reasons for quitting will actually make a difference. Can you think of a specific time when you were able to resist the urge to smoke for a short period of time, even if it was just for a day or two?

turns.user_message: I think so... there was this one time when I was on a plane and I forgot my cigarettes in my other bag, and I didn't have any cash to buy more at the airport. step2_rag_chunks: If you slip and smoke a cigarette, it does not mean you have failed. Many people attempt quitting several times before succeeding.
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

A relapse does not erase the progress made. The most useful question after a slip is not why did I fail but what was happening just before â€” what was the trigger, what was the situation. That specific information is what makes the next attempt more informed. Most people who successfully quit smoked again at some point during the process.

step2_rag_chunks: Taking a short walk or doing light physical activity can help reduce stress and distract from cravings.
Talking to someone about your cravings can help reduce the intensity of the urge.

There are two kinds of triggers â€” situational and emotional. Situational triggers are external: after meals, with tea, during a work break. Emotional triggers are internal: stress, boredom, loneliness, anxiety. Most people have both. Knowing which type is stronger for you helps us choose the right approach.
